‚ÄãConcordance vs. Comorbidity‚Äã
‚ÄãConcordance: relatives tend to share the same disorder at higher-than-chance rates.‚Äã
‚ÄãComorbidity: two disorders occur together in the same person more often than expected.‚Äã
‚ÄãSimilarity: both suggest some kind of relationship.‚Äã
‚ÄãDifference: concordance is about shared disorder in relatives; comorbidity is about two disorders in one individual.‚Äã
‚ÄãExpected comorbidity if independent:‚Äã‚ÄãP(A and B) =‚Äã‚ÄãP(A) √ó P(B)‚Äã
‚ÄãCalculating observed comorbidity as follows:‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãOverall comorbidity rate = percent of all people with comorbid A&B = number_of_people_with_comorbid_A&B /‚Äã
‚Äãtotal_number_of_people_in_whole_set‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãComorbidity rate in disease A = percent of people with disease A who have comorbid A&B =‚Äã
‚Äãnumber_of_people_with_comorbid_A&B / total_number_of_people_with_A‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãComorbidity rate in disease B = percent of people with disease B who have comorbid A&B =‚Äã
‚Äãnumber_of_people_with_comorbid_A&B / total_number_of_people_with_B‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãAssuming that one of the diseases always comes first, we will simplify our lives in this class by considering the two most common‚Äã
‚Äãexplanations for a correlation between two diseases:‚Äã
‚Äã1)‚Äã
‚ÄãA single set of genes gives rise to risk of both disease 1 and disease 2. People may get one, both, or neither.‚Äã
‚Äã2)‚Äã
‚ÄãDisease 1 causes increased risk for disease 2.‚Äã
‚Äãa)‚Äã
‚ÄãGenes increase risk for Disease 1.Getting Disease 1 increases the chance of Disease 2. Not everyone with Disease‚Äã
‚Äã1 gets Disease 2, just more than expected.‚Äã
‚Äãb)‚Äã
‚ÄãIf some people still get Disease 2 alone ‚Üí there must be separate genes for Disease 2.‚Äã
‚Äã3)‚Äã
‚ÄãDisease 2 causes increased risk for disease 1. Same idea as above. Can be ruled out if Disease 1 always occurs first.‚Äã
‚ÄãTesting the hypothesis that TS and OCD have common genetic risk factors (Pauls & colleagues)‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãLook at relatives of probands to identify people who may carry risk genes without necessarily showing both disorders.‚Äã
‚Äãcompare with controls who don‚Äôt have risk genes‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãRelatives of probands with nephritis only (no colon cancer) ‚Üí have nephritis risk genes but no nephritis‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãControls ‚Üí no family history of nephritis or colon cancer‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãWhy these groups:‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãIsolates genetic risk (genes only) without the effect of actually having nephritis‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãPredictions: If shared risk genes ‚Üí relatives (no nephritis) have higher colon cancer rate than controls, If nephritis causes later‚Äã
‚Äãcancer ‚Üí relatives (no nephritis) have same (or lower) colon cancer rate as controls‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãKey detail to include: Must measure colon cancer rate in relatives WITHOUT nephritis, Must use probands who had nephritis‚Äã
‚Äãbut NOT colon cancer‚Äã
‚ÄãHow to identify specific risk genes (or, more precisely, specific alleles, or versions, of genes that correlate with a disease and thus are‚Äã
‚Äãlikely to increase risk).‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãCollect genomic data for people with disease vs. without disease‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãProtective alleles appear more often in people without the disease.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãDifferent alleles can have different effect sizes.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãGenetic overlap across disorders can confirm earlier family-study conclusions.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãHow you can then confirm a study like the Pauls study‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãSpecifically, the Pauls study first demonstrated that OCD and TS must have a large overlap in the set of alleles that‚Äã
‚Äãcause risk but didn‚Äôt identify any specific genes. But then decades later finding alleles for TS and for OCD and seeing‚Äã
‚Äãa large overlap confirmed that finding‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãVocal Learning & Autism‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãModular hypothesis of vocal learning‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãVocal Coordination: Chorusing, turn-taking, rhythmic entertainment‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãEx. Gibbons chorusing, echolocation, human sign language‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãVocal Production Variability: Babbling, critical periods of learning‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãEx. birds babbling to learn how to sing specific songs‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãVocal Versatility: Acoustic repertoire complexity, pitch matching, imitation, mimicking‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãEx. songbird with syrinx, humans with lips and tongue‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãAutism Spectrum Disorder‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãPrevalence: 1/32 of population. Developmental disability caused by brain differences, diagnosed starting at three‚Äã
‚Äãyears old. Comorbid with anxiety, depression and ADHD‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãSymptoms limited eye contact, sensory stimuli sensitivity, difficulty with social communication‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãTreatments: speech therapy, occupational therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy. Medications: SSRIs, antipsychotics,‚Äã
‚ÄãADHD stimulants‚Äã

‚ÄãParkinson‚Äôs Disease‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãSecond most common neurological disorder.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãCaused by degeneration of dopamine neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta (SnC).‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãSymptoms: tremor, rigidity, reduced movement, shuffling gait.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãLoss of SnC dopamine causes basal ganglia dysfunction.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãSnC: movement, VTA: mood and attention‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãOther DA-related disorders: ADHD, depression, anxiety, OCD, Tourette‚Äôs, addiction.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãL-dopa / dopamine replacement,‚Äã‚ÄãTaken several times‚Äã‚Äãa day.Often works only for 5‚Äì10 years. Can cause major side effects.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãDeep brain stimulation,‚Äã‚ÄãEffective only while stimulation‚Äã‚Äãis on. Masks symptoms but does not repair underlying degeneration.‚Äã
‚ÄãAddiction‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãReceptor compensation:‚Äã‚ÄãThe brain adjusts to repeated‚Äã‚Äãdrug use (e.g., reduces receptor number or sensitivity) to maintain‚Äã
‚Äãbalance.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãTolerance:‚Äã‚ÄãProlonged agonist use causes‚Äã‚Äãdownregulation (fewer GABA receptors)‚Äã, so there are fewer receptors for the‚Äã
‚Äãdrug to act on ‚Üí‚Äã‚Äãweaker signal ‚Üí reduced effect ‚Üí tolerance develops.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãWithdrawal:‚Äã‚ÄãDrug is stopped ‚Üí normal GABA release but‚Äã‚Äãfewer receptors‚Äã‚Äã‚Üí‚Äã‚Äãless inhibitory GABA signaling than‚Äã
‚Äãbaseline‚Äã‚Äã‚Üí brain becomes‚Äã‚Äãoveractive‚Äã‚Äã‚Üí symptoms like‚Äã‚Äãanxiety, agitation, seizures‚Äã.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãCocaine‚Äã: Increases dopamine signaling. Tolerance/withdrawal are linked to compensation in dopamine receptor number, not‚Äã
‚ÄãDAT.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãOpioids and Pain‚Äã: Opioids reduce pain by inhibiting glutamate pain signals entering the spinal cord.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãOpioids and Reward:‚Äã‚ÄãOpioids increase dopamine release from the‚Äã‚ÄãVTA to nucleus accumbens‚Äã.‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãImportant circuit: GABA neurons in VTA normally inhibit dopamine neurons. Opioids inhibit those GABA neurons. This‚Äã
‚Äãdisinhibits dopamine neurons, so dopamine release increases.‚Äã
‚ÄãACC‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãReduce the emotional/affective component of pain (how unpleasant it feels)‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãProduce analgesia and relief‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãCan also contribute to reward/pleasant feelings‚Äã
‚ÄãFibromyalgia: Fewer receptors ‚Üí‚Äã‚Äãless natural pain inhibition ‚Üí heightened pain experience‚Äã, which matches fibromyalgia‚Äã
‚Äãsymptoms‚Äã
‚ÄãAnxiety & Depression‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãBoth diseases also have same biomarkers (changes in stress hormones, changes in shape and activity in various brain areas)‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãBoth diseases have almost 100% identical genetic risk factors, high comorbidity‚Äã
‚Äã-‚Äã
‚ÄãThis set of correlations doesn‚Äôt prove that they are caused by a common set of biological changes but all of them are‚Äã
‚Äãconsistent with the idea that a common set of biological causes both‚Äã
‚ÄãSerotonin (5-HT):‚Äã‚Äãmonoamine (not catecholamine), broken down by MAO‚Äã
‚Äã5-HT1:‚Äã‚Äãinhibitory, calming, low compensation‚Äã
‚Äã5-HT2:‚Äã‚Äãstress-related, complex effects, high compensation‚Äã
‚ÄãMDD:‚Äã‚Äãlow mood, anhedonia, ¬± psychosis‚Äã
‚ÄãGAD:‚Äã‚Äãexcessive persistent anxiety‚Äã
‚ÄãPTSD:‚Äã‚Äãtrauma, flashbacks, hyperarousal‚Äã
‚ÄãEpisode vs Disorder:‚Äã‚Äãsingle episode vs recurring condition‚Äã
‚ÄãMeds:‚Äã‚ÄãSSRI, SNRI, NRI, MAOI (‚Üë monoamines)‚Äã
‚ÄãSSRI hypothesis:‚Äã‚Äã‚Üë serotonin immediately, mood improves later ‚Üí due to receptor compensation‚Äã
‚Äã5-HT2:‚Äã‚Äãblocking serotonin improves mood (‚Üì stress effects), fits compensation model‚Äã
‚ÄãEnvironmental Causes:‚Äã‚Äãepigenetics/stochastic development, as discussed in class.‚Äã
‚ÄãAntagonist:‚Äã‚Äãa substance that‚Äã‚Äãbinds to a receptor but blocks its activity‚Äã, preventing the normal effect of a neurotransmitter or‚Äã
‚Äãhormone. NALOXONE. LESS SIGNAL,‚Äã‚Äã# of receptors:‚Äã‚Äã‚Üë (upregulation),‚Äã‚ÄãEffect on signal:‚Äã‚Äã‚Üë sensitivity over time‚Äã
‚ÄãAgonist:‚Äã‚Äãsubstance that‚Äã‚Äãbinds to a receptor and activates it‚Äã, producing a biological effect (mimics the natural‚Äã
‚Äãneurotransmitter/hormone). MORPHINE. MORE SIGNAL,‚Äã‚Äã# of receptors:‚Äã‚Äã‚Üì (downregulation)‚Äã‚ÄãEffect on signal:‚Äã‚Äã‚Üì over time (tolerance)‚Äã
